concept: a heap lives in an array; index math links parents and children
cue: Use the child formulas: from index i, the two children sit at 2 i plus 1 and 2 i plus 2.
note: A binary heap is a flat array. The smallest value sits at index 0, and a parent at index i is never larger than its children at 2i+1 and 2i+2. Here the parent 3 is no larger than its children 7 and 9.

concept: sift up climbs a new item while it is smaller than its parent
cue: The new value 2 starts at the end; swap it with its parent whenever the parent is larger, and stop once the parent is no larger.
note: Sifting up is how push keeps a heap valid: a new item climbs until its parent is no larger. The climb is at most the height of the heap, so O(log n) swaps.

concept: push adds an item then sifts it up
cue: Add val with this.data.push, then let it climb: while it is smaller than its parent, swap the two and set i to the parent index.
prompt: A MinHeap keeps its smallest value at the front, stored in the array `this.data`. Complete `push(val)` so it appends `val` and then sifts it up until every parent is no larger than its children. `pop`, `peek`, and `size` are already written. Return `this`.

cases.setup: const h = new MinHeap(); [5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2].forEach(x => h.push(x)); const out = []; while (h.size() > 0) out.push(h.pop());
const h = new MinHeap(); [4, 4, 4].forEach(x => h.push(x)); const out = []; while (h.size() > 0) out.push(h.pop());

note: One push does O(log n) work: the new item climbs at most the height of the heap.

concept: pop removes the front, then sifts the moved item down
cue: The front value 1 leaves; the last value moves to the front and sinks, each step swapping with its smaller child.
syntax: compare data[l] and data[r] to pick the smaller child
note: Pop returns the smallest value (the front), then repairs the heap by sinking the moved item past its smaller child, O(log n) steps.

concept: pop removes the front then sifts down
cue: Save data[0] to return. Move the last item to the front, then sink it: keep swapping with the smaller of its two children until neither child is smaller.
prompt: Complete `pop()` on the MinHeap so it removes and returns the smallest value, which is always at the front. Move the last item to the front and sift it down past its smaller child until the heap property holds again. Return `undefined` for an empty heap. `push`, `peek`, and `size` are already written.

cases.setup: const h = new MinHeap(); [5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2].forEach(x => h.push(x)); const out = []; while (h.size() > 0) out.push(h.pop());

note: Pop is O(log n): the sunk item drops at most the height of the heap.

concept: popping repeatedly yields values in sorted order
cue: Each pop removes the current smallest, so reading them out one by one gives an ascending list.
note: A min-heap works as a priority queue: push in any order, and popping always returns the smallest value that remains.

concept: a size-k min-heap keeps the k largest values seen so far
cue: Whenever the heap grows past k, pop the smallest; the front is then the k-th largest of everything pushed.
note: Keeping only k items makes each push and pop O(log k), so a full scan is O(n log k) time and O(k) space.

concept: kth largest with a size-k min-heap
cue: Push each number; whenever the heap holds more than k, pop the smallest. The value left at the front is the answer.
prompt: Write `kthLargest(nums, k)` that returns the k-th largest value in `nums`, counting from the largest, so k=1 is the maximum. Use the `MinHeap` (already defined) holding at most k values.
note: Each push and pop is O(log k) on a heap of size k, so the whole pass is O(n log k) time and O(k) space, cheaper than sorting all n values.
mapsTo: LC 215 · Kth Largest Element in an Array

concept: k smallest by popping a heap of every value
cue: Push every number into the heap, then pop k times; each pop hands back the next smallest in order.
syntax: for (let i = 0; i < k; i++) out.push(h.pop())
prompt: Write `kSmallest(nums, k)` that returns the `k` smallest values of `nums`, sorted ascending. Push all values into the `MinHeap` (already defined), then pop `k` times.
note: Building the heap is O(n log n) here (n pushes) and each of the k pops is O(log n), so the result comes out already sorted.

concept: top k frequent via a size-k heap keyed by count
cue: Count each value first, then keep a size-k heap of the highest counts; break ties toward the smaller value so the output order is fixed.
syntax: pack the count and the value into one number before pushing
prompt: Write `topKFrequent(nums, k)` that returns the `k` values that appear most often. Assume every number is an integer from 0 to 999. Return them sorted by count descending, and for equal counts by value ascending, so the result is fully determined. Use the `MinHeap` (already defined).
note: A size-k heap keeps the pass at O(n log k). This MinHeap compares plain numbers, so the reference packs each (count, value) into one number that orders by count first and value second.

concept: negating values turns a min-heap into a max-heap
cue: Each stone is pushed as its negative, so the smallest stored number stands for the heaviest stone; negate again on the way out.
note: This MinHeap always pops the smallest number. Pushing negatives flips the order, so popping returns the largest original value: a max-heap for free.

concept: smash the two heaviest stones using a max-heap
cue: Store negatives so each pop gives the next heaviest. Take the two heaviest; if they differ, push the negative of their difference back; repeat until one or zero stones remain.
prompt: Write `lastStoneWeight(stones)` that repeatedly takes the two heaviest stones and smashes them: if they weigh the same both are destroyed, otherwise the lighter is destroyed and the heavier keeps the difference. Return the weight of the last stone, or 0 if none remain. Use the `MinHeap` (already defined), storing negatives so the smallest number is the heaviest stone.
note: Each smash does a constant number of O(log n) heap operations, so the whole process is O(n log n).
